refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of load→travel→dump cycle time against customer-defined targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report cycle time data, and (2) the operational achievement of a target—the latter is a performance outcome dependent on operator skill, site conditions, and equipment capability working together, not a single system function. The refined FR isolates the system's responsibility: providing visibility into cycle time performance. Achievement of the target (p95 ≤ CT_target) is a verification criterion applied to the integrated system under duty cycle, not a functional requirement of NGHE alone. This keeps the FR in the problem domain (what data/visibility is needed) and avoids prescribing how the target will be met.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports cycle time metrics (load start, travel start, dump start, dump end timestamps) with sufficient accuracy and latency to allow Site Manager and Equipment Operator to assess performance against CT_target. Verify under controlled duty cycle that reported p95 cycle time matches independent measurement.
sub_frs: Capture cycle phase transition events (load initiation, travel initiation, dump initiation, dump completion) with timestamp and accuracy sufficient for cycle time calculation.
Calculate and store cycle time duration for each load→travel→dump sequence.
Report aggregated cycle time statistics (mean, p95, p99) to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Equipment Operator) with configurable time windows.
Provide traceability linking reported cycle time to operational context (operator ID, site conditions, equipment state) for root cause analysis.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument load-bearing structure and dump mechanism with displacement/force transducers; aggregate raw signals into a time-series event log; define state-machine transitions (load-detect → travel-detect → dump-detect → return-detect) based on threshold crossings; compute cycle time as elapsed time between consecutive load-detect events; compare p95 percentile against CT_target via periodic statistical analysis.

DP2: Deploy independent event-capture subsystem (mechanical switches or proximity detectors) at three critical points (load zone, travel zone, dump zone); wire events to a time-stamping aggregator; reconstruct cycle phases from event sequence; calculate cycle time as wall-clock delta between load-event timestamps; report p95 via rolling-window statistics.

DP3: Embed a distributed observer network (multiple independent measurement nodes) at load, travel, and dump stations; each node records local timestamps and phase signatures; synchronize clocks via a common reference; fuse observations into a central event timeline; derive cycle time from timeline gaps; compute and report p95 percentile.

DP4: Instrument the primary load-path (e.g., hydraulic pressure line or structural strain point) with a single high-fidelity transducer; apply signal processing (filtering, feature extraction) to identify cycle phase boundaries from characteristic waveform signatures; timestamp phase transitions; calculate cycle time and p95 from transition deltas.

DP5: Integrate cycle-time measurement into the vehicle's existing telematics or control system (if available); leverage native sensor fusion and timestamping; define cycle phases via control-state machine or operator input; report aggregated statistics to a cloud or on-site dashboard; ensure measurement logic is decoupled from control logic via a dedicated measurement module.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument load-bearing structure and dump mechanism with displacement/force transducers; aggregate raw signals into a time-series event log; define state-machine transitions (load-detect → travel-detect → dump-detect → return-detect) based on threshold crossings; compute cycle time as elapsed time between consecutive load-detect events; compare p95 percentile against CT_target via periodic statistical analysis.

potential_sources: Transducer placement and calibration must be independent of vehicle model variants to avoid design fragility.

Threshold-crossing logic for state transitions could couple to site-specific conditions (e.g., soil density, load mass variation); mitigation: make thresholds configurable and validated per duty cycle.
Time-series event log aggregation assumes synchronized clock across all transducers; clock drift or jitter could introduce systematic bias in cycle-time calculation.
Statistical analysis (p95 computation) assumes sufficient sample size and stationarity; short measurement windows or transient operational changes could violate this assumption.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 is selected because it provides a clear logical separation between signal acquisition (transducers), event detection (state machine), and reporting (statistical analysis). This structure supports modular implementation and allows independent validation of each layer. The use of threshold-based state transitions is solution-neutral and does not prescribe specific sensor types or communication protocols. Coupling risks are manageable through configurable thresholds and clock synchronization discipline.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Transducers can be reliably mounted on load-bearing structure and dump mechanism without interfering with normal operation or safety.
ASSUMPTION: Threshold values for state-machine transitions can be determined empirically or from engineering analysis and remain stable across the agreed duty cycle.
ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (temperature, vibration, electromagnetic interference) do not degrade signal quality below acceptable SNR for reliable phase detection.
ASSUMPTION: Cycle definition (load-detect → return-detect) is unambiguous and does not overlap with partial-load or multi-load scenarios.
RISK: If transducers are sensitive to environmental factors (e.g., temperature drift, moisture ingress), calibration drift could cause false state transitions and systematic bias in p95 estimates.
RISK: If threshold-crossing logic is too sensitive, noise spikes could trigger spurious phase transitions; if too insensitive, genuine phase boundaries could be missed.
RISK: Clock synchronization failure or jitter could introduce measurement uncertainty larger than the required precision for p95 comparison.
RISK: If sample size is small or duty cycle is highly variable, p95 percentile may not be statistically stable, leading to unreliable acceptance decisions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Measurement precision requirement (e.g., ±5% cycle time accuracy), (2) Minimum sample size for p95 validity (e.g., n ≥ 100 cycles per reporting period), (3) Environmental operating range for transducers and electronics, (4) Definition of 'cycle' in edge cases (e.g., partial loads, idle periods, multi-load sequences), (5) Reporting frequency and latency (e.g., p95 updated every 1 hour, available within 5 minutes of query). Additionally, establish a validation protocol to confirm that threshold-crossing logic correctly identifies phase transitions under representative site conditions before deployment. Consider adding a secondary validation mechanism (e.g., operator confirmation or video correlation) for initial commissioning to build confidence in the measurement system.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized instrumentation, signal aggregation, state-machine logic, and cycle-time analytics for load-bearing structure monitoring and dump mechanism performance tracking

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit raw transducer signals, state transitions, cycle-time metrics, and p95 percentile statistics to remote monitoring/command center

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive CT_target threshold updates and configuration changes for statistical analysis parameters

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts when p95 cycle-time exceeds CT_target or anomalous state transitions detected

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying transport for Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert message delivery

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: instrumentation, signal aggregation, state-machine logic, and statistical analytics are core NGHE responsibilities. Transducer hardware integration and raw signal ingestion are localized. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces cross boundary, but these are expected external communication channels, not logic diffusion.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns physical structure and mechanical systems, not sensor signal processing or analytics. State-machine logic and cycle-time computation would be orphaned from vehicle control logic. High diffusion: telemetry, tasking, and safety logic would leak into Vehicle, violating separation of concerns.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is for remote command/control of vehicle operations, not instrumentation or performance analytics. Placing signal aggregation and state-machine logic here conflates operator interface with sensor processing. High diffusion: analytics and threshold comparison belong in a monitoring/diagnostics layer, not teleoperation.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns alert generation and safety-critical decision logic, but not the upstream instrumentation, signal aggregation, or state-machine definition. Placing the entire DP here would overload Safety Services with non-safety-critical analytics. Moderate diffusion: only the SafetyAlert output naturally belongs in Safety Services; the bulk of signal processing and cycle-time computation would be misplaced.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of integrated instrumentation, signal aggregation, state-machine logic, and performance analytics. It encapsulates the complete data pipeline from raw transducer signals through statistical comparison against targets, with clean external interfaces for telemetry export, tasking input, and safety alert generation. This placement preserves cohesion (all signal processing and analytics logic stays together) and minimizes coupling (external interfaces are well-defined and limited to expected communication channels).
containment_risks: If NGHE Solution does not already own transducer hardware drivers or signal conditioning, integration with Vehicle's sensor layer may require careful interface design to avoid tight coupling.
State-machine definition and threshold parameters must be configurable via Tasking interface; hardcoding thresholds in NGHE Solution would reduce flexibility.
If Safety Services has pre-existing alert generation logic, duplication of threshold-crossing detection may occur; recommend SafetyAlert interface be the single point of alert emission.
Periodic statistical analysis (p95 computation) requires buffering and windowing of cycle-time samples; ensure NGHE Solution has sufficient memory/compute allocation for this.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via the DP's statistical analysis pipeline. Raw transducer signals → state-machine transitions → cycle-time computation → p95 percentile comparison provides an auditable, repeatable measurement chain. Worksite safety plan integration is addressed via SafetyAlert interface. Verification is feasible provided transducer calibration, threshold tuning, and sample buffering are documented.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: NGHE Solution is the clear owner. Instrumentation (transducers), signal aggregation, state-machine logic, and cycle-time analytics form a cohesive functional unit with 0.92 containment score. External interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout) are well-defined and do not leak core logic into other modules. Allocation is unambiguous.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP fully addresses FR. The DP specifies: (1) instrumentation of load-bearing structure and dump mechanism with displacement/force transducers; (2) aggregation into time-series event log; (3) state-machine definition with four explicit transitions (load-detect → travel-detect → dump-detect → return-detect); (4) cycle-time computation as elapsed time between consecutive load-detect events; (5) p95 percentile statistical analysis; (6) comparison against CT_target. This end-to-end pipeline directly enables measurement and reporting of load→travel→dump cycle time against customer-defined targets. No functional gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Transducer hardware drivers and signal conditioning: If Vehicle module owns sensor hardware but NGHE Solution owns signal processing, interface contract must be explicit (e.g., raw ADC values vs. conditioned analog signals). Tight coupling risk if driver changes propagate into NGHE state-machine thresholds.

Threshold parameter management: State-machine transitions (load-detect, travel-detect, dump-detect, return-detect) depend on displacement/force threshold values. If these are hardcoded in NGHE Solution, Tasking interface cannot reconfigure them without code changes. Recommend externalizing thresholds as configuration parameters.

SafetyAlert duplication: If Safety Services module already owns threshold-crossing detection for other safety-critical functions, cycle-time threshold logic may duplicate. Recommend SafetyAlert interface be the single emission point; NGHE Solution computes, Safety Services emits.

Statistical analysis buffering: p95 percentile computation requires windowing (e.g., last N cycles or time-window). Memory allocation and garbage collection must be managed; if NGHE Solution runs on resource-constrained hardware, buffer overflow or latency spikes could occur. Recommend specifying buffer size and overflow policy.

State-machine definition clarity: Four transitions (load-detect → travel-detect → dump-detect → return-detect) are listed, but edge cases are not addressed: What if dump-detect occurs without travel-detect? What if return-detect is missing? Recommend explicit state-machine diagram with invalid transition handling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. The minor diffusion (SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces) is expected and appropriate; these are external communication channels, not logic leakage. Alternative module placements (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all score poorly (0.25–0.4 containment, 0.6–0.75 diffusion), confirming NGHE Solution is the correct owner. Cohesion is high: all signal processing, state-machine logic, and analytics remain together. Coupling is low: external interfaces are well-defined and limited to expected communication channels (telemetry export, tasking input, safety alert generation, network transport). No significant architectural risk identified at DP↔IM bridge.

recommended_revisions: Externalize state-machine thresholds (load-detect, travel-detect, dump-detect, return-detect displacement/force values) as configuration parameters accessible via Tasking interface. Hardcoding thresholds reduces flexibility and prevents field tuning.

Define explicit state-machine diagram with all valid and invalid transitions, including edge cases (e.g., dump-detect without travel-detect, missing return-detect). Specify error handling and recovery logic.

Specify transducer hardware interface contract between Vehicle and NGHE Solution: raw ADC values, sampling rate, signal conditioning (if any), and calibration metadata. Document coupling points to enable independent evolution.

Clarify SafetyAlert ownership: recommend NGHE Solution compute threshold-crossing events and emit via SafetyAlert interface; Safety Services receives and processes alerts. Avoid duplicating threshold logic.

Specify p95 percentile computation window (e.g., last 100 cycles, last 1 hour, sliding window). Document buffer size, overflow policy, and memory allocation. Ensure NGHE Solution has sufficient compute/storage.

Add acceptance criterion detail: specify transducer accuracy/calibration requirements, state-machine threshold tuning procedure, and p95 sample size (e.g., minimum 30 cycles per measurement window) to ensure statistical validity.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR↔DP, DP↔IM, FR↔IM) pass with high confidence, and the 0.92 containment score confirms NGHE Solution is the correct architectural owner. However, the gate results identify six material diffusion risks and edge-case gaps that, if left unresolved, will propagate into detailed design and create rework during implementation. Given high safety criticality, these risks must be addressed before decomposition proceeds. The recommended revisions are not cosmetic; they affect state-machine correctness, parameter flexibility, and interface contracts. Revising the current level (FR, DP, IM) to incorporate these clarifications will reduce downstream risk and enable cleaner sub-FR derivation.

revise.why: The DP currently specifies the state-machine logic at a high level ('four explicit transitions') but does not address: (1) threshold parameterization (hardcoded vs. configurable), (2) edge-case handling (invalid state transitions, missing events), (3) buffer management for p95 computation (window size, overflow policy), or (4) interface contracts with Vehicle (transducer signal format, calibration metadata). Revising the DP to include these details will eliminate ambiguity and reduce diffusion risk. Specifically: externalize thresholds as configuration parameters, define explicit state-machine diagram with error handling, specify p95 windowing strategy, and document transducer interface contract. This revision does not change the core solution concept but makes it implementable and verifiable.

assumptions: Transducers (displacement/force) are owned by Vehicle module; NGHE Solution receives conditioned or raw signals via a well-defined interface contract.
State-machine thresholds (load-detect, travel-detect, dump-detect, return-detect) can be externalized as configuration parameters and tuned in the field without code changes.
SafetyAlert interface is the single emission point for threshold-crossing events; NGHE Solution computes, Safety Services processes.
p95 percentile computation uses a sliding or fixed-window buffer; buffer size and overflow policy are specified and resource-constrained hardware can accommodate.
Transducer calibration, sampling rate, and signal conditioning are documented and stable across the product lifecycle.
State-machine edge cases (e.g., dump-detect without travel-detect, missing return-detect) are rare but must be handled gracefully to prevent false cycle-time reports.
open_risks: Threshold parameterization: If thresholds remain hardcoded, field tuning is blocked and Tasking interface cannot reconfigure. Risk: inflexible system, customer dissatisfaction, potential safety misclassification.

State-machine edge cases: Four transitions are specified, but invalid transitions and missing events are not addressed. Risk: undefined behavior, false cycle-time reports, safety alert generation errors.

Transducer interface coupling: If Vehicle module owns hardware drivers and NGHE Solution owns signal processing, tight coupling on signal format/calibration could prevent independent evolution. Risk: rework if transducer hardware changes.

SafetyAlert duplication: If Safety Services module already owns threshold-crossing detection, cycle-time threshold logic may duplicate. Risk: inconsistent alert generation, maintenance burden, potential safety gaps.

Buffer overflow under load: p95 computation requires windowing; if buffer overflows or garbage collection causes latency spikes, cycle-time measurement accuracy degrades. Risk: unreliable telemetry, customer complaints.

Statistical validity: p95 percentile requires sufficient sample size (e.g., ≥30 cycles); if sample size is too small or window is too short, p95 estimate is unreliable. Risk: acceptance criterion cannot be verified with confidence.
evidence_needed: Revised DP with explicit state-machine diagram (UML state chart or equivalent) showing all valid transitions, invalid transitions, and error recovery logic.
Configuration parameter specification: list of externalized thresholds (load-detect, travel-detect, dump-detect, return-detect), data types, ranges, and Tasking interface access method.
Transducer interface contract: signal format (raw ADC, conditioned analog, digital), sampling rate, calibration metadata, and ownership boundary between Vehicle and NGHE Solution.
SafetyAlert ownership clarification: confirm NGHE Solution computes threshold-crossing events and emits via SafetyAlert interface; Safety Services receives and processes. Document interface schema.
p95 percentile computation specification: window type (sliding, fixed-time, fixed-count), window size, buffer allocation, overflow policy, and minimum sample size for statistical validity.
Acceptance criterion refinement: specify transducer accuracy/calibration requirements, state-machine threshold tuning procedure, p95 sample size, and measurement repeatability criteria.
Edge-case handling specification: document behavior for dump-detect without travel-detect, missing return-detect, and other invalid transitions. Specify error logging and recovery.
